content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

April 15, 1996

Arthur Danner III
Santa Cruz County District Attorney
P.O. Box 1159
701 Ocean Street
Santa Cruz, CA 95061

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-96-109

Dear Mr. Danner:

This is in response to your letter requesting follow-up
advice regarding your responsibilities as District Attorney for
Santa Cruz County under the provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").1

Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

1. May you repay a personal loan that you made to your
committee in connection with your election in 1994?

2. May you continue to raise funds for expenses associated
with your 1994 election?

3. May you raise funds for your 1998 election and use those
funds to pay your litigation expenses?

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-96-109
Page 2


4. Can you make a loan to your committee to pay outstanding
legal debts and repay yourself at a later time?

CONCLUSIONS

1. You may repay a personal loan that you made to your
committee in connection with your election in 1994.

2. You may continue to raise funds for expenses associated
with your 1994 election, including paying litigation costs or
paying outstanding loans.

3. You may raise funds for your 1998 election and use those
funds to pay officeholder expenses, including your litigation
expenses.

4. You may loan funds to your committee to pay outstanding
legal and other debts. As noted above, loans may be repaid.

FACTS

The facts are substantially the same as that set forth in
your prior request for advice (Danner Advice Letter, No. A-96-
039). You are currently a defendant in a lawsuit filed by Ed
Frey, an unsuccessful opponent in your 1990 and 1994 elections.
The lawsuit alleges that you authorized, as district attorney, the
payment of regular salary to an attorney in the Santa Cruz
District Attorney's Office whom you placed on mandatory home leave
pending termination of her employment.

In the Danner Advice Letter, supra, we advised that payments
received to pay for attorney's fees in connection with the
litigation would be considered campaign contributions and that you
may use those funds to pay for your litigation expenses. You now
are inquiring about the proper way that these contributions should
be handled.

In your letter of March 18, 1996, you stated that you
campaigned for your current term of office in 1994 and that the
committee established for that campaign was still active. You
stated that you have not had any fundraising events since your
election in June of 1994. You also stated you loaned the
committee $5,000 for the election and have not yet been repaid.

ANALYSIS

Officeholder Accounts

An elected official currently holding office may maintain his
or her campaign bank account used to gain election to the term of
office for the purpose of collecting funds specifically to defray
the costs of the election campaign for his or her current term of

Our File No. A-96-109
Page 3


office and the costs of holding that term of office.² (Section
85201; Regulations 18520 and 18521.) This would include repaying
campaign loans (General Advice Letter, No. A-91-021) as well as
paying litigation expenses that may be paid with campaign funds.³
(Section 89514.)

Moreover, the Act does not set a specific time frame for
raising funds. Thus, an officeholder may continue to raise funds
for the officeholder account for these types of expenses.
However, where a candidate has more than one campaign bank
account, written solicitations for contributions must specifically
identify the campaign committee for which the funds are sought,
and should instruct the contributor to designate the campaign bank
account and specific office to which the funds are contributed.
(Regulation 18523.1.)

Contributions received must be deposited in the campaign bank
account for which the contribution is designated. If a
contribution to a candidate does not specify to which committee it
has been contributed, the candidate may deposit the contribution
in the campaign bank account of his or her choice. (Regulation
18523.)

Finally, where a candidate loans his or her own committee
personal funds, the loan is considered a contribution. Thus, you
may also loan your committee funds, to be repaid at a future date.

Future Election Campaign Bank Accounts

Under your facts, both the debts in question pertain to your
1994 election, or the term of office you hold as a result of that
election. Section 85201 provides that all campaign expenditures
for a specific election must be made from the campaign bank
account established for that election. Thus, although a candidate
may have campaign bank accounts for numerous terms of office, the
candidate must make all expenditures associated with a specific
election from the appropriate campaign bank account established
for that election.


2 However, funds in the officeholder account may not be used for
future campaigns unless transferred to that future campaign bank
account. Or, alternatively, you may redesignate your officeholder
account for a future election by filing a Candidate Intention
statement (Form 501) for the future term of
office, and filing a new Form 502 which redesignates your
officeholder campaign bank account for the future election.

3 Section 89514 provides that campaign funds may be used for
attorney's fees and other costs in connection with administrative,
civil, or criminal litigation so long as they are directly related
to the candidate's or elected officer's activities, duties, or
status as a candidate or elected officer.

Our File No. A-96-109
Page 4


However, Regulation 18525 provides:

(a) An incumbent elected officer shall make
expenditures for the following campaign expenses
from the appropriate campaign bank account for
election to a future term of office:

* * *

(b) An incumbent elected officer may make
expenditures for purposes not enumerated in
subdivision (a) from either the campaign bank
account established pursuant to Government Code
Section 85201 for election to the incumbent term of
office or from a campaign bank account established
pursuant to Government Code Section 85201 for
election to a future term of office. This section
shall not be construed to permit an incumbent
elected officer to make expenditures from any
campaign bank account for expenses other than those
associated with his or her election to the specific
office for which the account was established and
expenses associated with holding that office.

Both the loan and the litigation in question relate to your
governmental duties associated with your current term of office.
Nevertheless, you may make these expenditures from either the
campaign bank account established for election to the incumbent
term of office or from a campaign bank account established for
election to a future term of office. Moreover, funds raised for
this purpose may be deposited in either bank account.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5660.⁴

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division


⁴ Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
